topic: Someone discovers their mother kept every school assignment
raw_output:
[story 1]

The cardboard box was heavy, smelling faintly of old pencil shavings and dust. Liam dragged it into the living room, the floorboards groaning under his weight. Inside, nestled among yellowed notebooks and crumpled worksheets, was an entire year’s worth of schoolwork. He stared at the sheer volume—tests, essays, diagrams—and a tight knot formed in his chest. He remembered the late nights he’d spent frantically searching for a specific history paper, only to find it neatly stacked on a high shelf, labeled with his name. "Of course," he muttered to the empty room. He picked up a math assignment, the ink slightly faded, and felt a sharp, bitter twist in his gut. It wasn't just the clutter; it was the implication: she had managed it all, perfectly preserved, while he’d been left scrambling. He dropped the box with a thud, the sound echoing his sudden, hollow feeling.

[story 2]

Sarah traced the spine of a textbook, her fingers brushing over pages filled with meticulously handwritten notes. The realization hit her like a physical blow: her mother had hoarded every single piece of homework, every graded paper, from kindergarten through high school. A slow, cold wave washed over her. She looked around the room, the familiar decor suddenly feeling suffocatingly curated. She picked up a particularly dense chemistry lab report. Her jaw tightened. She didn't want to feel this heavy, this sense of being overlooked, this constant reminder of unseen labor. She stood up abruptly, pacing the small space. "Why?" she asked the silence, her voice sharp, laced with an unfamiliar edge of accusation. The sheer organization of it all only amplified the feeling of imbalance.

[story 3]

Mark found the storage bin tucked behind the seldom-used linen closet. It was overflowing with paper—quizzes, term papers, even permission slips. He pulled out a stack of English assignments, the crisp paper feeling alien in his hands. A simmering irritation began to build behind his eyes. He remembered asking his mother, months ago, why he couldn't just keep his own records. The answer, unspoken but undeniable, was the collection. He ran a hand over the top of a stack of essays, his knuckles white. He felt a distinct urge to tear something up, to disrupt the perfect, preserved order she had created. He shoved the box back into the shadows, his breath coming in shallow gasps, the air suddenly thick and unpleasant.
